item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems more controversial than scp-579. Glad to add my upvote

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be sourced, and thus is assumed to be non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). As such, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This scip perfectly encapsulates a problem I have with certain articles. It lacks detail. From my perspective the Foundation needs to study an anomaly enough to both contain it and ensure it doesn't breach that containment. Short articles like this are missing key tests and data essential to containment. For example has any form of communication been attempted? The article suggests that the manifestation is non hostile to those it doesn't consider intruders. Another angle could be as the pellets or slugs demanifest at a specific point researchers could stand there and safely observe the entity and its behaviour. There's a wealth of potential here that seems to be untapped. A reluctant -1 with the hope of a resurrection rewrite or expansion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I apparently downvoted the original version of this, but it made me chuckle so sure, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm. Good idea. I'll take that under consideration.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know what might improve this? An interview with one of the previous tenants or the former security guard. Right now, this is kinda meh for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I imagine they decompose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder how many ways there are for a furious man to kill himself if he's handcuffed and has no survival instinct. Could he perhaps angry himself into a coronary? Choke on his own tongue?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If, subsequent to being captured and disarmed…

Would it be a good idea to cuff him, and station someone upstairs with him so he doesn't demanifest? Otherwise you have to battle a man with a gun every time you want to perform a test.

item.title: Re: BACKSTORY
item.content:encoded: I feel strangely smug about realizing there was a reason so many of the 'people parts' had "public intoxication" in common in the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the backstory a few posts up, not the article. You might be able to hint at the backstory if you find out that the Security Guard is identical the guy who used to be the security guard there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a small phenomenon with a gigantic backstory, which isn't communicated through the article.
I agree with Scan, this would work much better as a tale. As an SCP, it's a -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dont think I should have to read the comments to get to the part of this scp that would make me not downvote, so downvote.

That said, I absolutely love the story of Top Floor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one's simple and not very original, and yet I still kind of like it. Upvoted.

item.title: Re: BACKSTORY
item.content:encoded: I have changed my no vote to a downvote because of a few problems that I found after some thinking. My problem with your backstory is this: If this was a regular building, wouldn't there be records of who owned or worked in or rented the building? If the Foundation did their homework, they would get those records and investigate the businesses and people on the list. They could have found that one of the businesses had less than natural dealing or whatever it was that you said. By not implying any of this in the article, you make the Foundation look rather foolish because they took this SCP at face value when there was information that is easily found.

item.title: Re: BACKSTORY
item.content:encoded:
I don't particularly like this. Parts of it feel like a rehash of the Door (Door Top Floor is a good top floor. Door Top Floor does the things it does because that's what top floors do. Door Top Floor can't be a top floor because the top floor is what it is and if it were something else it couldn't be a top floor. Good top floor.) . Other parts of it I like (nitpicking chunks of people to get some trait with them, for example), but not enough to change my vote, and I can't vote based on something that isn't in the article anyway. And for the rest I just don't care.

That said, it's very nice to see backstories like this for skips one already likes, and I appreciate you providing more insight into your vision of the skip even when I personally don't care for it.

item.title: Re: BACKSTORY
item.content:encoded: Not bad, but eh. I like it as a security measure by warlock city council more.

item.title: Re: BACKSTORY
item.content:encoded: Voct, you have convinced me that you are quite possibly pretty much the best re-writer on the site, I somewhat wonder if you can redo an archived page

item.title: Re: BACKSTORY
item.content:encoded: Of course not. The Foundation doesn't know this. Always remember, the Foundation is — technically — an unreliable narrator.

item.title: Re: BACKSTORY
item.content:encoded: This would be good as a tale, but it's not evident from the article in any meaningful way.

item.title: Re: BACKSTORY
item.content:encoded: That is a bit better. However, even though I would upvote the idea of this, I can't really see it clearly reflected in the article itself. As such, I can't change my vote in good conscience quite yet.

item.title: Re: BACKSTORY
item.content:encoded:
…

Voct, I think I love you.

item.title: BACKSTORY
item.content:encoded:
Oh, I'm still figuring that out.

Shooty Man isn't sapient. As the Foundation correctly deduces, Shooty Man is just part of the anomaly. There is a sapience here, but the Foundation hasn't realized it, and thus there's nothing in the article that refers to it. "Sapience" isn't quite the right word, honestly, but human vocabulary lacks the necessary subtleties.

It's sort of a mind, sort of an imprinted personality, sort of fragments of a decaying after-echo of a determined will to accomplish a particular task. It's the building — or the top floor, rather. It — the mind-echo — wasn't there when the building was built, or during the initial years of the building's occupation. But then the recession hit, and the businesses that operated out of that building started to fail… and there were fewer and fewer people coming in every day… and then the company that owned the building failed, and the building was bought at a bankruptcy auction for a pittance, and then the purchasers went bankrupt and it was sold again…

Something was being done there. Something strange was being done, in one of those top-floor offices, by strange people who worked for one of those tiny companies that was struggling to keep its head above water while the sharks took bites from underneath. Even anomalous businesses have to pay for their office space, and as the economy worsened, there were fewer and fewer of the strange people working for the company that did the strange things, and fewer and fewer strange things for them to do.

Until one day, the strange little business collapsed, and its strange little employees were left unemployed. The business's assets were seized for non-payment of rent, and sold at yet another auction, its strange equipment bought by a scrap-metal merchant who could never have understood what he was melting down, its strange projects left forever unfinished…

… and yet.

Some of the employees at that strange little business had been on very good terms with the security guard. He'd been a very nice security guard, very friendly, holding doors for people if their arms were full of boxes, always taking the time to speak clearly and distinctly to people who had difficulties with English, and never losing patience with their utterly peculiar accents… the kind of security guard who'd be featured in a children's book about why the Security Guard Is Your Friend.

And even on the day that the strange people showed up for work and found that the office had been sealed for non-payment of rent and they weren't allowed in… the security guard was still very nice to them. He was polite, and friendly, and so apologetic, and he told them that he'd really love to let them in to get their stuff, but it was his duty, they understood duty, didn't they, he could get in trouble, the building's new owners were real jerks, they'd take any excuse to fire him, and he couldn't afford to lose his own job…

and the strange people understood about duty. They really did. They understood that being the security guard for that building had once been a reasonably pleasant job, but now was a horrible bitter unpleasant chore where you had to do all kinds of mean stuff and witness people's careers — their whole lives — disintegrating…

The strange people assured the security guard that they didn't hold it against him. They all shook his hand — some of them even tried hugging him — and they wished him well. And then they left the building, and he never saw any of them ever again.

A month later, the building's second owners went bankrupt, and sold it to a holding company for a fraction of what they had paid, and the holding company jacked up rents even more, and soon there were no tenants left, and at the very end even the security guard was laid off. The holding company tried to sell the building but no one was interested in buying it because the whole industrial park was pretty much desolate by now, and the holding company dissolved and everyone went their separate ways and the building was forgotten.

… and on the top floor, in that empty office suite that had once been occupied by a strange little business where strange people did strange things… something started to happen. Undetectable residue from strange things that had been done there, interacting with undetectable residue from other strange things, Very slowly, in the silence and solitude and strangeness, a personality began to emerge.

Why only the top floor? That's the only area that the personality really has any effect — at first, it was limited to the office suite where it began, but it gradually spread itself throughout the top floor as a whole. Could it go further if it tried? Maybe — but it won't. It's the personality of the top floor. Leaving the top floor would mean not being the personality of the top floor. And it's the personality of the top floor, so it can't not be the personality of the top floor, because then it wouldn't be the personality of the top floor, and it is the personality of the top floor. So don't ask stupid questions!

So the top floor is sitting there on top of the other floors, contentedly being the top floor. But something is missing — it needs a security guard. The top floor doesn't know very much, but it knows how important it is for a building to have a security guard. That's the only person who kept coming to the building in the end — the security guard. That means he was important. And a security guard is in an abandoned building to protect the top floor. So that means it's important to have someone there who protects the top floor.

The top floor doesn't know where security guards come from, though. So it decides to make one.

The top floor's mind reaches out all over the world, and it finds people. It copies the traits that it likes, and assembles them, and gets a very nice security guard… but then it realizes that this isn't right. A security guard should be depressed, and angry, and filled with guilt, and with just a bit of a drinking problem, just like the security guard who came to visit the top floor back when it was starting to develop its sense of self.

One problem, the top floor discovers, is that a good security guard — even one who's miserable in exactly the way the top floor thinks he should be — will eventually want to leave. So it has to fine-tune the personality traits a bit more — not by altering what it's using, but by copying from different sources. And it's easier to copy a chunk of someone's physicality and take the personality trait with the chunk, than it is to extract the trait from the chunk and copy the trait separately.

So eventually, the top floor has cobbled together a patchwork security guard. He's miserable, and filthy, and consumed by guilt and rage, and it's not a coincidence that so many of his components were copied from people who thought of themselves as failures. He's unable to think of a life beyond the top floor, or of anything beyond hunting down anyone who intrudes on the top floor while it's busy being the top floor. The top floor chose to give him a shotgun because it decided shotguns felt nice; it has a serial number from a different model of shotgun because the top floor liked that serial number better.

The top floor doesn't need him to exist all the time, though — just when it needs to be protected from intruders. So as soon as the intruders aren't a problem any more, it stops making him exist. And if for some reason, he accidentally leaves the area where the top floor is able to make him exist, then he stops existing, and the top floor makes him exist again in a new area. If he gets killed, then the top floor stops making the cadaver exist, and makes a new version exist elsewhere in its area.

If he gets captured, well… the top floor considers that he's still protecting the top floor from intruders as long as he's still being aggressively hostile to intruders.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I saw this post, I thought, "boy, this could make this interesting for me if the right context is provided and enough hints at what exactly is happening are given." I thought about it, and it didn't, unfortunately.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's an interesting point that's very difficult to notice in the article itself, because it's actually an absence.

Look at the tags. Something is missing.

"Sapient".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As I said in the PM, you piqued my curiosity when you brought up the identifying characteristics, but before I could even finish thinking "Oh, that's interesting" it was done. More can be done with this. I've removed my downvote, but I'm not upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am… Well, disappointed in this rewrite, to say the least. It doesn't look like you actually changed anything major in the article that would give it the oomph it needs. My downvote firmly remains.

item.title: Re: Mod Post
item.content:encoded: Voct has asked me to cancel the deletion vote, as it's -7 now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the clean simplicity of it but I feel it really needs a log entry of some kind to pull the article together. As it stands, the quilt-bodied attribute is a pretty cool thing but I find myself immediately scrolling down for just a little more. A log of some field testing in action might do this.

I'll upvote so the thing doesn't die before an addition of that spirit can be attempted. It feels like it's almost there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hobo with a shotgun jokes aside this is now an article that really does it for me, straight forward and simple.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is still best SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay. This is definitely more detailed and less stupid … but sorry to say, doesn't seem to do anything to me. Maybe I'm missing some obvious deduction, it being almost 5AM.
I think this downvote is there for the keeps. No vote after a bit of talk with Voct.

To explain further. The guardian of this building appears to be assembled out of the characteristics of a number of individuals, all of them under arrest, and all of them with all their relevant data in convenient police databases, that whoever made this probably also had access to.
Now, who else out there is connected to state agencies and has means and ability to create flesh golems/ simulacra? The city council from SCP-1047, that's who. And that's why my downvote went poof.

item.title: Re: Mod Post
item.content:encoded: Rewrite complete; thanks to EchoFourDelta for much guidance.

item.title: Mod Post
item.content:encoded:
I'm granting permission. You have until midnight on Friday. If a rewrite has not been posted by that time, deletion proceeding will progress as normal.

-timersnip- -rgt

item.title: Rewrite
item.content:encoded:
It's fallen to -10, and (with the help of Echo) I've worked out how to salvage this one.

May I have permission to do so?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really, really like this article.

E: Seriously, this is best SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to agree, simple articles can be the best. If the awkward wording were fixed I'd upvote this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why did I once upvote this article? There's nothing to it.

OH! I remember. There was a tale written about it which took it from bland to interesting.

That time has passed now. Unless the aspects of the tale are incorporated into the SCiP, I won't be continuing to support this article.

Vote Redacted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i agree with stecks, it just seems so lackluster compared to alot of similar things

item.title: Re: Actually
item.content:encoded: If you're wanting to play around with this, send a PM to Light. She's busy with school. I'll draw her attention to it the next time I see her, if you want to take a swing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoa! Stecks! Nice to see you back around, man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
!
Seconding Scanny's with "holy carp" , mainly because I prefer carps to shit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy shit, you're not dead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get why there are so many upvotes. It's a ghost that shoots you. So what?

item.title: Re: Just needs some touch-ups
item.content:encoded: Ty for telling me.

item.title: Re: Just needs some touch-ups
item.content:encoded: Remove your signature.

item.title: Just needs some touch-ups
item.content:encoded: You should try to make it a little fuller feeling, like add some stuff to it like a test log and stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love, I absolutely love, how simple it keeps everything. That said, there's a lot of potential here, it's just not being used to its fullest. I want to know more. What happens if you come in from the roof? What if there's multiple people? Why did it respond to the robot? What if the robot wasn't broadcasting? Can it be killed?

Other then that, there are some style issues that make it seem a bit informal or hard to read, but that's relatively easy to fix.

"At some point during the vacant period, entity SCP-1270-1 began manifesting whenever the building became occupied. SCP-1270-1 is identical to a human, specifically a human male carrying a shotgun, except that it does not exist when the building is unoccupied. Infrared and motion-sensing cameras confirm this."
^If it attacked the robot, how did you get the cameras in? Or get anyone in without confronting the SCP in more detail?

It also seems a bit.. awkward to read. An example of how I would phrase it would be something like

"At an unknown point after the abandonment of SCP 1270, the entity designated as SCP 1270-1 manifested within the building, according to survivors of previous encounters. SCP 1270-1 was described as, and later confirmed to appear as, a human male carrying a pump-action shotgun. Police heat-sensing equipment has confirmed that the entity is not present when the building is not occupied."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one based off of its simplicity, although I would want to see more of it, such would be:

Have they ever tried to apprehend SCP-1270-1?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm downvoting for now, because I just don't feel like this has enough to it. There's nothing wrong with what's already present, I just don't feel like it's quite enough to support an interesting article. I think I'd like to see the entity's behavior and motivations delved into, or even some sort of hint at a potential origin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
late response is late

At some point during the vacant period, Entity SCP-1270-1 began manifesting whenever the building became occupied.

That's what I mean by "tone feels off," just some bits of it don't quite feel very clinical to me. But perhaps that's just me.

item.title: Actually
item.content:encoded: No, on second thought that's not right. The tests are good, but the ultimate motive's not there yet. I can feel it, though. I really can.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, we could have some tests — drill a hole through the floor/ceiling and pipe a fiber-optic camera through, for instance — to ascertain that, yes, shooter-dude simply does not exist when there's not anyone else present. Throw in some tear gas and he's unaffected. Send in a robot and there's no one there.

We could also examine his shotgun pellets and find that they're identical each time (which I believe is impossible for shotguns). Perhaps the isotopic makeup of the pellets is completely wrong also.

More interestingly, I'm thinking about who the dude is, why he's there, what he's defending, and where he goes when he's not defending it.

He's hoarding something.

I'm thinking about SCP-1646, the worldwalker who's using (our/the Foundation's) reality as a dump site for his victims. And I'm thinking about compulsive hoarders.

I'm thinking that this guy is using the third floor to stash random garbage WONDERFUL TREASURE that he's collecting from all sorts of realities. He might go very far away in many dimensions, but he always stays near in at least one. And if anyone comes too close, he instantiates himself back and BLAM BLAM BLAM BLAM STAY AWAY MINE MINE MINE MINE.

Just a notion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dunno. I never liked this one. There's just nothing in there. An oh so mysterious shooter dude shooting people in an old office building.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was doing well when it was posted and I always enjoyed it. Strange that it's sinking into oblivion. Perhaps a potential rewrite could give more purpose to the guardian's presence, maybe tell us more about the building's history?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm pretty sure I see how to fix this, honestly.

Since no one's asked yet: Do tell?

(Personally I still feel entirely neutral about this article, so I'm curious.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm pretty sure I see how to fix this, honestly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's an abandoned building with a maniac inside and nothing else. It sounds like a lame campfire story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thinking on it, I'm really not that interested in the story that this tells.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is no emotion here, at all. Which is part of the issue; there's no emotion, no hook.

That isn't even remotely what I was talking about. If you don't think this evokes emotions (and that this is a problem), then you are making a separate point entirely.
